coredata.dc:title: From electrochemical flash sintering (EFS) to high-frequency flash sintering (HF-FS) of ionic conductors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Fabre, Timothée
Fournet-Fayard, Franck
Steil, Marlu Cesar
Bouchet, Renaud
coredata.dc:description: The development of all-solid-state electrochemical devices, such as batteries and solid oxide cells, is constrained by conventional high-temperature sintering, which is energy-intensive and limits material compatibility. In this work, we extend and generalize the Electrochemical Flash Sintering (EFS) to enable the single-step production of multilayered materials such as Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP)/ Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 (LATP)/ Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP). To address the inherent limitation of Flash Sintering (FS) of purely ionic conductors (LATP), namely, the requirement for reversible electrochemical reactions at the current collector/ceramic interface, we demonstrate that high-frequency (100 kHz) power pulse can trigger the flash event while bypassing interfacial constraints, regardless of electrode type. Experimental results show high densification in <10 s for LATP using blocking electrodes (Au). By decoupling the flash event from interfacial electrochemistry, this High-Frequency Flash Sintering (HF-FS) approach expands the range of compatible materials and applications, offering a rapid and electrochemically compatible route for fabricating advanced all-solid-state devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical flash sintering
High frequency-flash sintering
Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 (LATP)
Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP)
All solid-state systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample preparation: powders preparation and pellets shaping
LVP, LATP and composite LVP-LATP powders
Extension of EFS approach to multilayer LVP-LATP/LATP/LVP-LATP

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to gratefully acknowledge financial support from the French National Research Agency (Contract no. ANR-20-CE05-0040-02) and from the Centre of Excellence of Multifunctional Architectured Materials \u201CCEMAM\u201D.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impedance spectroscopy diagram of LATP green sample at 250 °C with gold blocking electrodes and indicated cutoff frequency fc. If f 〈 fc: there is no possible charge transfer induces strong capacitive blocking interface; if f 〉 fc: HF allows to avoid blocking electrodes and thus current to flow.

(a) Flash sintering of the thin multilayer sample at 1 kHz. (b) Impedance diagrams before and after flash at 100 °C. (c) Images of the sample after flash, top and side views. (d) Lateral cross-section of the sample by microtomography (voxel size = 5.30 µm).
SEM images (BSE) (a) × 100 of the entire multilayer system, (b) × 500 of the interface between the composite and the LATP, (c) × 10k within the LATP layer, (d) × 5k within the composite layer.
(a) STEM image of composite/LATP interface × 20k. (b) Superposition of STEM-EDX maps of titanium (violet) and vanadium (yellow). (c) STEM-EDX map of Ti. (d) STEM-EDX map of V.
(a) Impedance diagram of the LATP sample at 250 °C before flash (b) Zoom on the impedance diagram in the sample resistance region with measurement frequencies indicated.

(a) Evolution of current density and electric field during the flash. (b) Impedance diagrams of the sample before and after flash at 250 °C. (c) Evolution of the sample's conductivity before and after flash between 150 and 250 °C.

(a) Picture of the sample split in two after flash. (b) Top and lateral cross-sections of the sample after flash by microtomography (voxel size = 5.19 µm). (c) Diffractograms of the LATP powder and the sample dense zone after flash.
SEM images × 2k of the microstructure of the LATP sample densified by flash at 100 kHz in the densified region: (a) in a fracture and (b) with the polished surface.
Summary of experimental conditions used in EFS and HF-FS.

head.ce:title: From electrochemical flash sintering (EFS) to high-frequency flash sintering (HF-FS) of ionic conductors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Grenoble Alpes, University Savoie Mont Blanc, CNRS, Grenoble INP, LEPMI, 38000 Grenoble, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Grenoble Alpes, University Savoie Mont Blanc, CNRS, Grenoble INP, LEPMI, 38000 Grenoble, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of all-solid-state electrochemical devices, such as batteries and solid oxide cells, is constrained by conventional high-temperature sintering, which is energy-intensive and limits material compatibility. In this work, we extend and generalize the Electrochemical Flash Sintering (EFS) to enable the single-step production of multilayered materials such as Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP)/ Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 (LATP)/ Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP). To address the inherent limitation of Flash Sintering (FS) of purely ionic conductors (LATP), namely, the requirement for reversible electrochemical reactions at the current collector/ceramic interface, we demonstrate that high-frequency (100 kHz) power pulse can trigger the flash event while bypassing interfacial constraints, regardless of electrode type. Experimental results show high densification in <10 s for LATP using blocking electrodes (Au). By decoupling the flash event from interfacial electrochemistry, this High-Frequency Flash Sintering (HF-FS) approach expands the range of compatible materials and applications, offering a rapid and electrochemically compatible route for fabricating advanced all-solid-state devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Flash sintering (FS) is a process allowing to sinter materials in only few seconds, instead of hours-long conventional sintering thanks to the application of a simple and low-cost electric field to a preheated sample. It has been firstly shown with yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) material, an oxygen vacancy conductor [1]. This ultra-fast densification is made possible thanks to electrochemical reactions occurring at the interface between the electrodes, the material and O2 atmosphere [2], allowing the ionic current to flow through the green compact to heat it3. Since, it has been extended to a wider range of materials from other ionic conductors to electronic conductors, or semiconductors [4]. While thermal runaway and Joule heating are widely accepted as the primary drivers of flash sintering [5–9], the role of electrochemical reactions at the interface remains critical for ionic conductors. In the case of YSZ, these reactions involve oxygen exchange with the atmosphere, but purely cationic conductors (e.g., Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3) require alternative strategies to enable current flow.

The apparent ease of YSZ flash sintering in O2 atmosphere masks the complexity of flash sintering ionic conductor ceramics, requiring reversible electrochemical reactions to allow current flow. High DC electric field has been used to force the current flow through the interface (current collector/ionic conductor) leading to partial electrolysis of the material (i.e., oxidation at the positive & reduction at the negative electrode). The asymmetric electrochemical kinetics at the electrodes implied by DC electric field lead to dissimilar interfacial charge transfer resistance and consequently to asymmetric temperature profile through the sample [10]. It reflects in a nonlinear conductive behavior, indeed at the anode the oxidation of YSZ induces the formation of oxygen vacancies (main ionic charge carrier) and oxygen evolution, which increases the conductivity nearby [11] and generates inhomogeneities such as abnormal grain growth [12,13]. At the cathode, it leads to the blackening of the YSZ, due to its partial reduction. Blackening of YSZ has been studied from a long time [14–17] and different mechanisms have been proposed [18]. It was further described in the context of flash sintering [19–21].

The spatial extension of modifications (from the interface to the bulk) is proportional to the total charge passed, Q = ∫I(t)dt (as per Faraday’s law). In contrast, an AC field centered at zero limits permanent material modifications caused by electrolysis. Instead, the extent of these modifications depends on the duration of anodic or cathodic polarization (a function of frequency) and the reversibility of the electrochemical reactions. For reversible interfacial reactions, the sample returns to its initial state after each AC cycle (±), a principle that underpins our Electrochemical Flash Sintering (EFS) approach. Consequently, AC electrical fields (typically in the 10 Hz-1 kHz range) promote more reversible and controlled interfacial reactivity. This approach has been extensively demonstrated to prevent or minimize the severe structural and electrochemical degradation often associated with DC fields, as reported in multiple studies [2,13,19,22–28].

The use of an AC field instead of DC was also investigated using the purely Na⁺-conducting phase Na3Zr2(SiO4)2(PO4) with platinum electrode in dog-bone geometry [29]. Under high DC field, a strong sample electrolysis is observed, with reduction to metallic sodium at the cathode and oxygen evolution at the positive electrode (both accompanied by phase decomposition). Under high AC field at 800 Hz, the reactivity near the electrodes is less pronounced which confirms the interest of AC to minimize the extension of phase degradation at the interface. While in dog-bone geometry, the interfacial degradation could be disregarded in comparison to the bulk properties of the bone, in thin pellet/disc geometry with low L/S ratio it becomes of first importance.

With FS done in AC mode, reversible electrochemical reactions are necessary for current flow through ionic conductors without irreversible interfacial degradation. Caliman et al. brought to light the importance of the nature of the electrode, with the example of a purely cationic Na+ material (beta-alumina) [3]. In this case, the O2 atmosphere cannot be involved in the electrochemical reaction (e.g., Na+/Na) at the electrodes like with YSZ (O2 (atm.)/O2- (YSZ)). This means that flash sintering without material electrolysis is impossible with a blocking Pt electrode. However, using silver electrode has been shown to unlock FS, as it can be involved in the reversible electrochemical reactions at the interface Ag/b-Al2O3: Ag ↔ Ag+(b-Al2O3) + e- . The use of Ag electrode has been later reported with purely cationic Li+ conductors [30–36]. Unfortunately, it comes with silver contamination at the interface with the Ag electrode.

Another approach was assessed with Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 (LATP), a purely Li+ cationic conductor in Lachal et al. study [37]. It was shown that the blocking behavior of Pt electrode regarding a purely cationic conductor can be overcome by constructing a multilayer system with a mixed electronic/cationic electrode active material (AM), LiCoO2 (LCO), coating each side of the LATP green pellet. Such a system allows FS thanks to the Li+ (and electron) reversible electrochemical (de)intercalation reaction at the interfaces LiCoO2/LATP, i.e., LiCoO2 ↔ Li1-xCoO2 + x Li+ + x e- . This process can be termed electrochemical flash sintering (EFS) and represents a novel approach to design the architecture of a Li+ all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs), which are regarded as safe and sTable high-energy storage systems [38,39].

To further explore the versatility of EFS, we extend this approach to a multilayer system by replacing the conventional LCO electrode material with Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP). Unlike LCO, LVP offers a distinct advantage: it can function as both a negative and positive electrode [40,41], thereby paving the way for the development of functional ASSBs [42–44].

The primary objective of this work, however, extends beyond these material-specific advancements. We aim to demonstrate that the nature of the electrode (blocking or reversible) can be effectively circumvented by tailoring the frequency of the applied AC field. This hypothesis is supported by impedance spectroscopy analysis of LATP with gold current collectors at 250 °C (Fig. 1 ). The impedance diagram consists of a high-frequency (HF) semicircle corresponding to the material response, and a low-frequency capacitive tail corresponding to the response of the blocking gold electrodes [45]. The threshold frequency (ft) separates these regimes: below ft, the system is capacitive (no current flow); above ft, ionic migration dominates. By operating at frequencies > ft, we selectively activate ionic processes within the material, bypassing interfacial electrochemical constraints allowing the flash to occur. This breakthrough defines our high-frequency flash sintering (HF-FS) approach, unlocking new possibilities for the densification of ionic conductors. To date, only one study in the literature has explored the application of a high-frequency electric field (1 MHz) during field-assisted sintering of Li1.5Al0.5Ge1.5(PO4)3 (LAGP). In this work, the authors claimed that at this frequency, “the pellet faces act as a short circuit, inducing a current flow in the sample” [46]. However, this assertion lacks robust experimental validation to support the proposed behavior. In contrast, our study is the first to provide direct experimental evidence demonstrating that operating above the threshold frequency ft allows the flash process to proceed without being hindered by electrode limitations. This original approach offers a clear and reproducible solution to an important challenge in the flash sintering of pure cationic conductive ceramics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation: powders preparation and pellets shaping
LVP, LATP and composite LVP-LATP powders
ce:section.ce:para: Li3V2(PO4)3 (LVP) powder direct ceramic synthesis is described in our previous work [47]. Lithium carbonate (Li2CO3), vanadium oxide (V2O5) and NH4H2PO4 precursors were ground in mortar pestle for 2 h and pre-calcined at 350 °C for 5 h in an alumina crucible under argon + 10 % H2 atmosphere to remove gases compounds. The pre-calcined precursors were again ground in mortar pestle for 30 min before calcination at 850 °C for 12 h under argon + 10 % H2 atmosphere.

Li1.4Al0.4Ti1.6(PO4)3 (LATP) powder was provided by NEI Corporation. Mass loss of 2.3 % was estimated by TGA under air up to 700 °C (Mettler-Toledo with STARe software), with data provided in Fig. S1a of supplementary information (SI). Consequently, LATP powder was calcinated at 450 °C during 3 h under air to remove organic impurities.

LVP pristine powder and LATP calcinated powder were ground separately by planetary ball milling (Pulverisette 7 classic line, Fristch) at 400 rpm for 2 h in a zirconia jar with balls of diameter 3 mm and isopropyl alcohol (IPA). Particle size distribution was controlled by laser granulometry (Mastersizer 3000, Malvern Panalytical) with measured median particle diameter in volume (DV,50) of 0.8 µm and 0.7 µm for LVP and LATP, respectively. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of both powders after ball milling are provided in SI, Fig. S1b & c (Zeiss Ultra 55).

Composite LVP-LATP powder with 40LVP/60LATP (in wt %) formulation was prepared from the previously milled LVP and LATP powders. A wet-mixing approach was preferred to improve dispersion between the two powders. The powder mixture was first dispersed in IPA suspension using a magnetic stir bar and an ultrasonic probe (Bandelin Sonopuls HD 2200) to ensure proper dispersion and break up agglomerates. The suspension was then mixed for 1 h at 34 rpm in a three-dimensional shaker (Turbula T2F). Finally, the suspension was evaporated for 24 h at 60 °C while stirring with a magnetic bar to prevent any preferential sedimentation.

For monolayer LATP pellets, ball-milled LATP powder was compacted first with a uniaxial press under 40 MPa in a die of diameter 8 mm, then with an isostatic press under 250 MPa. Green compacts with a relative density of 63–65 % TD (theoretical density of NaSICON Li1.4Al0.4Ti1.6(PO4)3 = 2.94 g.cm-3) were obtained by geometrical measurements. The green compacts measure 7.60 mm in diameter (∼ 0.45 cm2 in area) and 2.40 mm in thickness after isostatic pressing. A thin gold layer of around 250 nm was deposited by sputtering (SC7620 Mini Sputter Coater) on the pellet surfaces.

Multilayer LVP-LATP/LATP/LVP-LATP pellets were prepared with a composite/LATP/composite stacking sequence, using the same amount of powder for each layer. Composite electrode powder was first placed into the 8 mm diameter die and pre-pressed uniaxially at 20 MPa. The second layer, LATP electrolyte, was added and pre-pressed at 20 MPa. Finally, the last composite electrode layer was added, and the entire stack was pressed at 40 MPa. As with the monolayer pellets, the multilayer green compact was then subjected to isostatic pressing at 250 MPa and finally reached 1.4 mm in thickness (61 % in average density). Similarly, a thin gold layer was sputtered on the pellet surfaces to optimize electrical contacts.

Flash sintering experiments have been done in isothermal conditions as described in a previous study [22], i.e., the samples are stabilized at a specific T0 temperature at which flash event will be triggered, with an associated effective conductivity σeff(T0) obtained by impedance spectroscopy. The furnace atmosphere is fixed to a pO2 of 5.10–6 atm thanks to an argon flux (8 L/h) for EFS of multilayer system and under air for HF-FS of LATP. Then a fixed electric field E0 is set until the flash event happens, after a certain time Δt. E0 value is chosen in order that power density dissipated by Joule effect (PJ = σeff(T0)·E0 2) exceeds an empirical threshold value of 10 W/cm3 required to trigger a flash [7,48]. Just after the flash event (abrupt rise of the current density), the generator switches from voltage control to current control. The current density is then fixed to a specific Imax value during a hold time thold. The pellet is placed between two Pt grids to enhance the electrical contacts and can be probed in-situ by impedance spectroscopy. Table 1 summarizes experimental conditions used for EFS of multilayer system and HF-FS of LATP pellet. For HF-FS, the temperature T0 of 250 °C was chosen so that the selected frequency of 100 kHz is superior to ft, as explained in Fig. 1.

The generator was developed by Galtenco Solutions (33,600 Pessac, France), with a power up to 10 A and 150 V. It is possible to adjust the frequency of the AC delivered signal from 1 kHz to 500 kHz, allowing high-frequency flash sintering (HF-FS). The operating principle is based on converting a DC source into AC by using MOSFETs arranged in an H-bridge, allowing the polarity to be switched extremely rapidly as a square wave signal. Image of the generator, theoretical representation of the signal and oscilloscope signal measurements at 100 kHz are presented in Fig. S2a, b & c respectively, in SI. To work with this HF power supply, a specific FS setup was designed. It consists of a miniaturized shielded heating chamber with the aim to reduce inductive losses due to reactance at high frequencies. Our original setup had an inductance L of 5.10–6H, and this value could be decreased by an order of magnitude to 5.10–7H for the specific HF-FS setup. The detailed diagram of the setup, as well as photos of the assembled setup, are shown in Fig. S3a & b respectively, in SI.

The LATP samples’ structure before and after HF-FS were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Data were collected from a PANalytical X'Pert Pro MPD diffractometer using q/2q Bragg-Brentano geometry with a diffracted beam Cu Ka radiation (l1 = 1.5406 Å and l2 = 1.5444 Å).

The microstructure was examined using a Field Emission Gun SEM (Zeiss Ultra 55) on powders and polished or fractured surface of the pellets after FS. SEM images were performed with secondary electrons (SE) and back-scattered electrons (BSE) at magnitude × 100, × 500, × 2k, × 5k and × 10k.

X-ray microtomography of densified pellets was performed with EasyTom XL tool from RX Solutions, using a micro source with an energy of 70 keV. The detector is a flat panel of 1536 × 1920 pixels with a pixel pitch of 127 μm. Volumes are reconstructed by filtered back projection with X-Act software from RX Solutions. Images are then processed with Fiji software.

Detailed observations of the densified multilayer system microstructure were carried out using a scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) with the JEOL JEM-2100F field emission instrument operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. This instrument enabled observations of thin sections at magnifications ranging from × 20k to × 200k, prepared via focused ion beam-scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM) using the Zeiss Crossbeam NVision 40 equipped with a gallium ion (Ga⁺) source.

Impedance spectroscopy was measured in temperature with a Solartron SI-1260. The impedance measurements were conducted in-situ within the furnace using the same electrodes, both before and after flash sintering. The excitation signal was set from 100 mV to 400 mV depending on the temperature and the resistance of the sample with frequencies varying from 107 Hz to 500 mHz. Measurements were recorded with Zplot software. Data (Nyquist diagrams) were analyzed with the Zview software (Scribner Associates Incorporated) to determine the conductivity from the total resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extension of EFS approach to multilayer LVP-LATP/LATP/LVP-LATP
ce:section.ce:para: Impedance measurements at 100 °C, conducted before and after the flash, shown in Fig. 2b, reveal that the bulk resistance of the multilayer system decreased by a factor of two (from 490 kΩ.cm to 240 kΩ.cm, respectively). Accordingly, the frequency at the summit of the bulk semicircle increased from 24 kHz to 40 kHz, consistent with the observed reduction in impedance. After the bulk material loop at 24 kHz, the low-frequency region exhibits a small bump between 250 and 300 kΩ.cm, followed by a straight line with a slope below π/4. This feature is characteristic of diffusion-limited charge transfer processes in composite electrodes [38,51] (here, LVP-LATP). Thus, this spectrum shows at low frequency the response of a composite electrode with Li⁺ charge transfer and mixed ionic-electronic conduction, which lies at the heart of both electrochemical flash sintering (EFS) and the one-shot elaboration of solid-state batteries.

Fig. 2c shows that densification is localized (black part corresponds to molten gold deposition), with one-half of the sample appearing to be densified and exhibiting characteristic cracking at its center. This indicates that current preferentially localizes, leading to heterogeneous densification of the object. Current localization and hot spot formation is a common problematic with FS process [49]. However, microtomography cross-section (Fig. 2d) reveals that more than half of the sample presents advanced densification (> 75 %).

Fig. 3a presents the SEM images (BSE) of the finely polished multilayer cross-section of the denser region, revealing the three densified layers thanks to the slight contrast in average Z between LATP and LVP. A large area with intimate interfaces can be observed, although significant porosity is present at the bottom of the image. A focused image of the interface between the composite and LATP is shown in Fig. 3b The thicknesses of the layers can be estimated at approximately 380 µm for the left composite layer, 375 µm for the LATP layer, and 350 µm for the right composite layer. In this area, the quality of the continuous interface between the two layers is demonstrated. Dense microstructures are observed in all layers (Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d). The electron beam reveals the microstructure but generates intergranular cracks, showing that the grain size is around one micron, which is close to the size of the initial particles. In the composite (Fig. 3d), more pronounced porosity is observed.

EFS originally shown with a LCO/LATP/LCO stack [37], is thus demonstrated again here with the LATP-LVP/LATP/LATP-LVP multilayer. Densification thanks to the EFS leads to the formation of a hot spot with different densification zones. The microstructural analysis shows that dense areas can be obtained both in the LATP and in the composite. Even, if the homogeneity of the sintering has to be improved with maybe a more controlled flash [52–54], densification of such a multilayered system paves the way of building all-solid-state battery by EFS.

A thin foil of this sample was prepared by focused ion beam (FIB) at the composite/LATP interface and analyzed using STEM-EDX to investigate atomic interdiffusion at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The foil thickness was estimated to be 150–200 nm. Fig. 4a displays the densified microstructure, where pores appear as bright regions. Fig. 4b shows an overlay of STEM-EDX elemental maps for titanium (Ti, purple) and vanadium (V, yellow). Ti exhibits high intensity in the LATP region and low intensity in the composite (Fig. 4c), whereas V is present with high intensity in the composite region and almost no intensity in the electrolyte region (Fig. 4d). These images reveal a near-homogeneous distribution of titanium and vanadium within the composite, indicating a possible reactivity between LATP and LVP. On the other hand, the superimposed Ti and V maps (Fig. 4b) clearly delineate the interface between the composite (left) and LATP (right). At this magnification (× 20,000), the boundary appears sharp, particularly in the V map, enabling unambiguous identification of the two layers. Notably, vanadium diffusion into the LATP layer is confined to a narrow region of a few hundred nanometers.

A green LATP pellet with a relative density of 64 % was prepared, with gold coatings deposited on both faces by PVD. It was placed in a specific HF setup, between two platinum electrodes. During a preheating cycle in air, it was heated to T0 = 250 °C. The impedance diagram of the sample at this temperature is shown in Fig. 5a . As expected, the bulk LATP electrical properties appear clearly in the high-frequency (HF) range as a loop, followed at lower frequencies by the response of the blocking gold electrodes, characterized by a strong capacitive rise. The total resistivity of the sample at 250 °C is 2310 Ω.cm. Fig. 5b shows a zoom of the impedance diagram around the transition region between the bulk LATP response at HF and the capacitive interface response at lower, i.e., around the threshold frequency ft. Different frequencies of interest are indicated. As explained in the introduction, the principle of High-Frequency Flash Sintering (HF-FS) of pure cationic ceramics relies on applying an electric field at frequency above ft, which effectively short-circuits the blocking electrode interface. In this context, the 1 kHz and 10 kHz frequencies lie on the capacitive (blocking) side of the impedance spectrum, where the electrode/electrolyte interface dominates the response. Conversely, the 100 kHz frequency shifts to the material’s bulk response side, enabling current to bypass the interfacial capacitance and promote the flash event. It is worth noticing that the characteristic frequency of the material shifts toward higher frequencies while the temperature (and densification) of the sample increases. This means that during the flash sintering process, the 100 kHz electrical response of the sample exhibits a transition toward the electrode/electrolyte interface. This phenomenon likely reduces the duration for which the electrode is not fully blocking during the flash discharge, particularly during the rapid temperature rise. At this frequency, the interface impedance, estimated as Z ≈ 1/ (2πCf) ≈ 0.15 Ω (with C ≈ 10⁻⁶ F), remains sufficiently low to avoid significantly impeding the flash process. These observations suggest that, despite the dynamic redistribution of the electrical response, the interface does not become a limiting factor for current flow during flash sintering.

At T0 = 250 °C, the effective conductivity σeff is 0.43 mS.cm-1. An electric field E0 of 200 V/cm at a frequency of 100 kHz is applied using the home-made HF power supply (see in SI Fig. S2c the I, E generator curves at 100 kHz). A higher electric field is used here compared to EFS (Section 3.1) to compensate for the lower effective conductivity at T0. Here, the theoretical power PJ is 17.2 W/cm3 (> 10 W/cm3). Fig. 6a shows the flash sintering (FS) process under these conditions, triggered after 9 s. The electric field E drops significantly from around 200 V/cm to 50 V/cm, with a sharp transition occurring during the flash event while the current density is set at 4 A/cm². Shortly after the flash (around 10 s), a rebound in the electric field is observed. This behavior is similar to that observed for the EFS (Fig. 2a) and can be attributed to the loss of contact in the hotspot region.

Fig. 5b shows the impedance of the green pellet at 250 °C before and after the flash. A substantial reduction in total resistance from 2310 Ω.cm to about 180 Ω.cm is observed. From impedance spectra obtained before and after the flash event (in-situ measurements in the furnace), the ionic conductivity was calculated and plotted in an Arrhenius graph (Fig. 6c). Before the flash, the green sample exhibits an activation energy of 0.61 ± 0.05 eV, with its electrical behavior dominated by interparticle contact resistance. This relatively high value reflects the temperature-dependent increase in ion mobility and the modifications of grain-to-grain connectivity with de temperature. Following the flash process, the calculated activation energy is 0.25 ± 0.05 eV. While this value-based on only three data points-should be interpreted with caution, it aligns well with literature reports for dense LATP samples, where total activation energies typically range from 0.20 to 0.40 eV [55]. This suggests improved ionic transport pathways post-flash, likely due to enhanced densification during the ultra-rapid sintering process. These results demonstrate that applying an AC electric field at 100 kHz enables flash sintering of LATP samples with blocking electrodes by effectively bypassing the interfacial electrode/green pellet impedance.

The sintered sample shown in Fig. 7a exhibits a clean fracture surface with a localized hot spot concentrated in one-half. Both fragments were jointly characterized using microtomography, with two representative cross-sections presented in Fig. 7b: a transverse section (top) and a lateral section (bottom), corresponding to the yellow guide lines. The primary hot spot region appears clearly in the upper-left quadrant as a lighter grey area, indicating higher density. A secondary, smaller hot spot is also visible in the bottom-right region of the transverse section. This localized densification is accompanied by structural inhomogeneities, including stress-induced cracks and circular porosity concentrated at the hot spot's core. Such porosity could be due to gas formation (such as Li2O(g) [56]) resulting from the very high temperatures likely reached at the core of the hot spot during the flash (> gold melting temperature at 1064 °C). The lateral section further reveals significant shrinkage in the densified region compared to the surrounding non-densified material. The pellet was cut to isolate the dense zone (hot spot region), which density was measured using Archimedes’ principle at 92 %. Fig. 7c compares X-ray diffraction patterns between the initial LATP powder and the flash-processed dense zone (after gold coating removal), confirming complete retention of the LATP phase with only residual gold from the original electrode layer.

SEM observation on the fractured surface of the microstructure of the densified sample region (92 %) reveals an intergranular fracture surface (Fig. 8a ), characteristic of a well-consolidated microstructure with fine grains ranging from 1 to 2 µm in size. Examination of the polished surface (Fig. 8b) confirms the high degree of densification, though residual porosity remains visible. Quantitative porosity assessment proves challenging due to grain pull-out artifacts introduced during sample preparation.

The rapid, high-quality densification of multilayered ceramics remains a critical challenge for advanced energy technologies, including all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) and solid oxide cells (SOCs). In this work, we expand the capabilities of electrochemical flash sintering (EFS), demonstrating its potential to fabricate in one shot monolithic ASSB prototypes in seconds, a method adapTable to other all-solid systems. We demonstrate EFS in LVP-LATP multilayer systems using bifunctional LVP electrodes, with STEM-EDX confirming minimal interfacial diffusion, a critical requirement for maintaining electrochemical stability in ASSBs.

In the case of high frequency flash sintering (HF-FS), by operating at frequencies exceeding the threshold frequency (ft) of the material’s impedance response, bulk ionic migration is activated, bypassing the need for reversible reactions at the electrode interface. The flash event takes place and hot spot region in LATP achieve up to 92 % densification within 10 s. By eliminating the need for reversible electrode reactions, HF-FS broadens FS compatibility across a wider range of ionic conductors, including those previously incompatible with conventional FS methods. This electrode-independent, ultra-fast densification method opens new possibilities for fabricating monolithic ASSBs with tailored microstructures and interfaces. While HF-FS demonstrates significant promise, further optimization of frequency and power density will be essential to extend its applicability to a broader range of ionic conductors and device architectures.

Timothée Fabre: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Franck Fournet-Fayard: Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Marlu Cesar Steil: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Renaud Bouchet: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to gratefully acknowledge financial support from the French National Research Agency (Contract no ANR-20-CE05-0040–02) and from the Centre of Excellence of Multifunctional Architectured Materials “CEMAM”.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2026.148192.
